What We Monitor / Archimedes Screws

Asset type

Archimedes screw monitoring

The lower bearing sits submerged in wastewater. The screw turns at 20–80 RPM through a high-ratio gearbox. The site is unmanned, the MCC is the only access point, and a failure means a pollution event. Most Archimedes screws have zero condition monitoring.

Talk to an engineer See what we detect

Why Archimedes screws are hard to monitor

Submerged, slow-turning, and remote

Archimedes screws at wastewater inlet works are the first line of defence against sewage overflow. When they fail, raw sewage bypasses screening and enters rivers or coastal waters. The most expensive failure is the submerged lower bearing, also the hardest to monitor. It sits underwater in abrasive wastewater, inaccessible for inspection, operating at speeds too low for standard vibration analysis.

€300K+

Lower bearing replacement cost including crane hire, civil works, and weeks of downtime. The most expensive single failure mode on any screw installation.

£90M+

Southern Water's 2021 fine for sewage pollution: the largest in UK water industry history. Thames Water faces £104M. Pump failure is a direct path to enforcement.

40–70%

Estimated share of smaller water utilities still relying on manual inspection and reactive maintenance for their screw pumps. No vibration routes. No online monitoring.

How ESA monitors Archimedes screws

A torque sensor at the MCC: no access to the wet end required

ESA measures current and voltage at the motor control cabinet. Every mechanical fault that changes the torque on the drivetrain (bearing wear, belt degradation, unbalance, debris loading) modulates the motor current. SAM4 reads those modulations continuously, without any sensor on the screw itself.

Belt system detection

Most Archimedes screws use belt drives from motor to gearbox. Belt frequency, harmonics, and pulley order are clearly visible in the current spectrum. Belt degradation, misalignment, and pulley wear produce distinct signatures. This is ESA's strongest detection area on these assets.

Torque through the gearbox

The gearbox attenuates vibration but amplifies torque. A 30:1 reduction ratio multiplies torque ripple by ~28×. Screw-side faults that create torque modulations (bearing wear, debris loading, imbalance) propagate to the motor current as sidebands around the supply frequency.

Fleet-scale remote monitoring

A large water utility operates 50–200+ screws across dozens of unmanned sites. ESA's cost structure (CTs at the MCC, no per-asset sensors, centralised analysis) makes fleet deployment economically viable where vibration routes would be prohibitive.

What SAM4 detects on Archimedes screws

Seven fault types from one sensor location

Based on 27 resolved condition incidents across Yorkshire Water, Anglian Water, and DuPont installations. Detection confidence is evidence-rated. We show what we can prove.

Fault type What's happening How SAM4 detects it Typical lead time Confidence
Belt degradation Wear, stretch, or cracking in the belt drive from motor to gearbox Belt frequency and harmonics in the current spectrum. Pattern changes as belt tension and surface condition degrade Instantly–weeks Full
Belt misalignment Pulley misalignment from installation error, thermal expansion, or wear Eccentricity and rotational frequency components related to belt and pulley geometry Step change Full
Mechanical unbalance Screw imbalance from debris buildup, worn flights, or shaft bow 1× rotational frequency in the current spectrum. Peer comparison across similar assets identifies anomalous units Months Full
Motor stator/rotor faults Insulation breakdown, rotor bar cracking from thermal cycling, moisture ingress, or high starting currents Current imbalance between phases, negative sequence current rise, and characteristic (1±2s)f sidebands for rotor bar faults Months Full
Motor bearing degradation Motor-side bearing wear producing rotor eccentricity Gradual increase at ½Fr and bearing fault frequencies in the current spectrum Weeks Conditional *
Supply frequency anomalies Power quality issues, VFD anomalies, or electrical supply degradation Supply frequency component changes detected through peer comparison. Flagging assets behaving differently from similar screws at the same site Weeks Full
Pulley unbalance Pulley wear, buildup, or damage in the belt drive system Belt frequency components modulated by pulley rotational frequency Months Full

 * Conditional: detects bearing faults producing rotor eccentricity. Sensitivity limited for driven-side faults beyond the gearbox. Detection capability depends on drive topology, sampling rate, load variation, and operating conditions. See ESA detection constraints.

Detection boundaries

No condition monitoring technology detects everything. These are the known limitations for ESA-based monitoring on Archimedes screws.

Low-speed screw shaft faults. The screw rotates at 20–80 RPM. Fault frequencies on the screw shaft (bearing defects, unbalance) fall at 0.3–1.3 Hz. Below ESA's reliable resolution. Motor-side faults at 1500 RPM are detected normally; driven-side faults below the gearbox are the challenge.
Gearbox internal faults. Large reduction ratios (30:1 to 60:1) attenuate high-frequency signals. ESA provides gearbox screening through torque modulation, but gear tooth and gearbox bearing diagnosis requires oil analysis or vibration on the gearbox housing.
Screw trough wear. Gradual abrasive wear of the trough increases leakback and reduces efficiency. This process doesn't produce a distinct torque signature. Visual inspection or physical gap measurement remains the only reliable method.
Flight weld cracking and corrosion. Small cracks and surface corrosion don't modulate torque until they become catastrophic. Annual visual inspection and ultrasonic NDT remain the standard for detecting structural flight damage.
Variable water level. Continuously changing inlet water level changes the load, which changes the current signature. Load-normalised baselines are required to distinguish mechanical degradation from operating condition changes.

Based on 27 resolved Archimedes screw condition incidents. Overall miss rate: ~11% (3 of 27 CIs). Misses were fault-specific, not systematic. The same assets had successful detections on other occasions. See full detection transparency report →

Monitoring architecture

What ESA covers. What it doesn't. Where inspection still matters.

The real comparison on Archimedes screws is not ESA vs vibration. It's ESA vs nothing. Most screws have zero online condition monitoring. SCADA catches complete failures but not developing faults. ESA fills the gap between run-to-failure and full-instrumentation programmes that most utilities can't justify.

ESA leads
  • Belt system faults (degradation, misalignment, pulley wear)
  • Motor electrical faults (stator winding, rotor bars, power quality)
  • Motor-side bearing trends from eccentricity signatures
  • Mechanical unbalance via peer comparison across the fleet
  • Remote 24/7 monitoring from MCC, no site visit required

ESA's strongest application on Archimedes screws is the belt drive and motor. Where fault signatures transmit cleanly to the current spectrum.

Both contribute
  • Motor bearing assessment: ESA detects eccentricity, vibration confirms severity staging
  • Gearbox screening: ESA sees torque modulation, oil analysis provides wear metal detail
  • Overload/debris detection: ESA sees load increase, SCADA confirms process conditions

When ESA and SCADA data corroborate, diagnostic confidence increases. ESA provides the spectral detail; SCADA provides the process context.

SCADA / inspection leads
  • Gearbox internal fault diagnosis (oil analysis, vibration on housing)
  • Screw trough wear (physical gap measurement)
  • Flight weld cracking (visual inspection, ultrasonic NDT)
  • Flight corrosion and biological fouling (visual during dewatering)
  • Submerged bearing seal condition (sealed-for-life designs eliminate this)

Annual inspection remains essential for structural faults that don't produce electrical signatures. ESA doesn't replace inspection. It fills the 364 days between inspections.

ESA monitors the motor and belt system. For complete drivetrain coverage, combine with periodic on-site inspection.

A water utility with 80+ Archimedes screws across multiple sites cannot justify vibration routes to every remote inlet works. ESA provides continuous fleet-wide monitoring at a cost structure that scales. It catches belt faults, motor degradation, and unbalance. The faults that develop between annual inspections.

For critical installations (flood defence, storm overflow), layer ESA with quarterly oil sampling on the gearbox and annual visual/NDT on the flights. ESA becomes the continuous baseline; inspection provides the structural confirmation. Detecting an anomaly from the MCC still beats discovering a €300,000 bearing failure during an emergency dewatering.

Screw detections

Real faults caught on Archimedes screws

From 27 resolved condition incidents across UK water utility installations.

Belt system Yorkshire Water

Belt harmonic and unbalance at inlet screw pump

Increase in belt harmonic combined with mechanical unbalance detected on an inlet screw pump at a wastewater treatment works. Belt system fault caught before failure. Demonstrates the primary detection use case for belt-driven Archimedes screws.

Motor bearing Anglian Water

Gradual bearing degradation on inlet screw

Gradual increase at ½Fr and possible bearing frequency detected on an inlet screw. Progressive fault development tracked over weeks. Demonstrates SAM4's ability to trend slow-developing motor-side bearing issues on low-speed drivetrains.

Peer comparison Yorkshire Water

Supply frequency anomaly identified via fleet comparison

Significant difference detected around supply frequency compared to similar screw pumps at the same site. Peer comparison method identified an anomaly invisible from a single-asset trend alone. Shows the fleet monitoring advantage on multi-screw installations.

Remote SPS Yorkshire Water

Fault detected at unmanned sewage pumping station

Sewage screw pump fault detected at Bridge Lane SPS, a remote, unmanned site with no other condition monitoring. ESA provided the only early warning. This is the core value proposition: monitoring the unmonitored.

Installation on Archimedes screws

Under 60 minutes. No screw access required.

1

Open the motor control cabinet

SAM4 installs at the MCC, the same panel your electricians already access. No confined space entry, no process disruption. Critical for remote pumping stations where site access is limited.

2

Clip sensor onto motor supply cables

Current and voltage sensors clip directly onto existing cabling. No wiring changes. No interruption to screw operation. Works with both DOL starters and VFD-driven screws.

3

Connect and commission

The SAM4 gateway connects via cellular (4G/LTE). No dependency on your IT network. Monitoring starts immediately. First diagnostic results within 48 hours.

Full installation guide → Security & network architecture →
